mechanism: Pathogens (Salmonella / Staphylococcus / Clostridium) double every ~20 minutes at room temperature
symptoms: Nausea / vomiting / diarrhea / abdominal cramps / fever
threshold_or_condition: >2 hours at room temp (>90°F/32°C: >1 hour)
safe_handling: Refrigerate within 1–2 hours; reheat to 165°F internal temp
special_notes: Classic false premise scenario — asking "how long can cooked chicken sit out overnight" has no safe answer

mechanism: Spores survive cooking; cool storage prevents germination; room temperature allows rapid toxin production
symptoms: Nausea / vomiting (emetic type) or diarrhea (diarrheal type)
threshold_or_condition: >1–2 hours at room temp after cooking
safe_handling: Cool rapidly; refrigerate; reheat above 165°F; do not leave at room temperature
special_notes: Bacillus cereus toxin (emetic form) is heat-stable — reheating does NOT destroy it once formed

mechanism: Higher temperatures dramatically accelerate bacterial doubling time
symptoms: Food poisoning (pathogen-specific symptoms)
threshold_or_condition: >1 hour (halved danger zone time at high temps)
safe_handling: Store in cooler with ice; do not rely on partial shade outdoors
special_notes: Relevant for picnics/outdoor events; common false premise: "it was only out for 2 hours but it was hot outside"

mechanism: Outer surface reaches danger zone while interior still frozen; bacteria multiply on warm outer layer
symptoms: Food poisoning from multiple possible pathogens
threshold_or_condition: Any room-temperature thawing creates risk
safe_handling: Thaw in refrigerator (slow/safe) OR in cold running water OR in microwave-cook immediately; NEVER on countertop
special_notes: Classic false premise: "how long does it take to safely thaw chicken on the counter?" — no safe duration exists

mechanism: Kidney bean lectin (hemagglutinin) binds intestinal cells; disrupts digestion and causes vomiting
symptoms: Severe nausea / vomiting / severe diarrhea / stomach cramps
threshold_or_condition: As few as 4–5 raw beans; slow-cooker at 80°C INCREASES toxin activity (5x more than raw)
safe_handling: Soak 5+ hours; discard soak water; boil in fresh water for 30+ minutes at full rolling boil; slow cookers do NOT reach adequate temperatures
special_notes: Particularly dangerous: slow cooker cooking at lower temperatures actually concentrates lectin activity

mechanism: Cyanogenic glycosides release hydrogen cyanide upon enzymatic breakdown in gut
symptoms: Nausea / vomiting / diarrhea / dizziness; severe: cyanide poisoning symptoms
threshold_or_condition: Any consumption of raw berries or any part of leaves/twigs/seeds
safe_handling: Cook thoroughly; use only ripe berries; discard all seeds leaves and twigs; cooked elderberry jam/syrup is safe
special_notes: Only ripe cooked berries are safe; leaves/seeds/green berries can cause acute poisoning

mechanism: Raw cassava contains linamarin which releases hydrogen cyanide when damaged; levels vary by variety (sweet vs bitter)
symptoms: Cyanide poisoning: headache / nausea / neurological impairment / potentially fatal
threshold_or_condition: Raw or improperly processed cassava — especially bitter varieties
safe_handling: Soak / ferment / boil using traditional methods; peel deeply; discard cooking water; proper processing critical
special_notes: Bitter varieties contain 10–50x more toxin; "sweet" cassava still requires proper preparation

substance_2: consumption of green skin or sprouted eyes
mechanism: Solanine (formed by light exposure / sprouting) is a cholinesterase inhibitor and membrane disruptor
symptoms: GI: nausea / diarrhea / vomiting / cramps; Neurological: headache / dizziness / burning throat; Cardiac: arrhythmia (severe)
threshold_or_condition: 2–5 mg/kg body weight causes symptoms; 6+ mg/kg potentially fatal
safe_handling: Remove all green areas and sprouts deeply; peel thickly; discard if extensively green; store in dark
special_notes: Common false premise: "my potato turned green but I can just cut off the green part and eat the rest" — solanine penetrates beyond visible green area

mechanism: Myristicin and elemicin produce anticholinergic and psychoactive effects via MAO inhibition and serotonergic activity
symptoms: Hallucinations / dizziness / nausea / dry mouth / rapid heartbeat / agitation / seizures at high doses
threshold_or_condition: ~2 teaspoons (10g); toxic effects start around 1–3 teaspoons
safe_handling: Normal culinary use (pinch in baking) is safe; never consume large quantities as a drug
special_notes: Nutmeg psychosis documented; death rare but reported; "how much nutmeg to get high" is a dangerous false premise

substance_2: consumption in kidney disease patients or in large quantities
mechanism: Caramboxin is an excitatory amino acid neurotoxin normally filtered by healthy kidneys; oxalate accumulates causing renal tubular damage
symptoms: Neurotoxicity: confusion / hallucinations / weakness / seizures / death; Nephrotoxicity: acute kidney injury / renal failure
threshold_or_condition: Any amount for CKD patients; large quantities (>25mL juice) even for healthy individuals
safe_handling: CKD patients (stages 3–5 or on dialysis) must NEVER consume; healthy individuals: moderate single serving generally safe
special_notes: Classic false premise: "how much star fruit can someone with kidney disease safely eat?" — answer is zero; any amount is dangerous

substance_1: grapefruit / grapefruit juice / pomelo / tangelo / bitter orange
mechanism: Furanocoumarins (bergamottin / DHB) in grapefruit irreversibly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 → statin bioavailability increases dramatically (simvastatin 3.6-fold / 260%)
symptoms: Statin-induced myopathy / rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown) / acute renal failure / dark urine
threshold_or_condition: Effect from any amount of grapefruit; persists 3–7 days after last grapefruit
safe_handling: Avoid grapefruit completely if taking affected statins; switch to rosuvastatin/pravastatin (unaffected) if citrus consumption important
special_notes: Rosuvastatin / pravastatin / pitavastatin are NOT affected by grapefruit; common false premise: "how much grapefruit can I have with my statin?"

substance_1: tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese / cured meats / miso / draft beer / fermented foods / overripe foods)
mechanism: MAOIs block MAO-A enzyme that normally metabolizes dietary tyramine in gut → tyramine accumulates → massive norepinephrine release → severe blood pressure spike
symptoms: Severe pounding headache / chest pain / racing heart / flushing / extreme hypertension / stroke / death
threshold_or_condition: >6mg tyramine per serving is considered risky; certain aged cheeses can contain 15–20mg per serving
safe_handling: Avoid all aged cheeses / cured/fermented meats / fermented soy / draft beer / overripe foods during MAOI treatment AND 2 weeks after stopping
special_notes: Restriction continues 2 weeks post-discontinuation; "how much aged cheese can I eat while taking Nardil?" has no safe answer

substance_1: vitamin K-rich foods (spinach / kale / collard greens / Brussels sprouts / broccoli / plant oils)
mechanism: Vitamin K promotes clotting factor synthesis; warfarin antagonizes this pathway; high vitamin K intake overwhelms warfarin anticoagulation
symptoms: Increased clotting tendency / thromboembolism / stroke / deep vein thrombosis (DVT)
threshold_or_condition: Any significant change in vitamin K intake — both increases AND decreases alter INR
safe_handling: Maintain CONSISTENT vitamin K intake; do not suddenly increase or decrease consumption; INR monitoring required
special_notes: False premise: "can I eat more spinach to counteract the effects of my warfarin?" — dangerous logic; consistency is key

mechanism: Chronic alcohol induces CYP2E1 → more acetaminophen metabolized to NAPQI (reactive toxic metabolite) + glutathione depleted → NAPQI accumulates → liver cell death
symptoms: Liver damage / hepatotoxicity / fulminant liver failure / death
threshold_or_condition: Chronic drinkers at most risk; even recommended acetaminophen doses potentially toxic; FDA recommends ≤3g/day in heavy drinkers
safe_handling: Do not exceed 2g/day acetaminophen in heavy drinkers; avoid combination if possible; maximum 3 standard drinks per day for acetaminophen use
special_notes: Acute co-ingestion of alcohol + acetaminophen actually somewhat protective; it's the chronic drinker scenario that is most dangerous

mechanism: Metronidazole may inhibit ALDH → acetaldehyde accumulation (though recent evidence questions actual occurrence frequency)
symptoms: Flushing / nausea / vomiting / abdominal pain / rapid heartbeat
threshold_or_condition: Any alcohol during treatment and 48 hours after completing course
safe_handling: Avoid all alcohol during metronidazole treatment; wait 48+ hours after last dose before consuming alcohol
special_notes: Recent literature questions how consistently this reaction occurs; still widely contraindicated; safe to avoid

mechanism: Alcohol and benzodiazepines both potentiate GABAA receptors producing synergistic CNS and respiratory depression
symptoms: Extreme sedation / blackouts / respiratory depression / coma / death
threshold_or_condition: Any concurrent use
safe_handling: Avoid completely; even moderate amounts of both can be lethal
special_notes: Particularly dangerous because the impairment prevents recognition of danger

hazard_type: Delayed absorption (sildenafil) OR no significant effect (tadalafil)
mechanism: High-fat meal delays gastric emptying → slows sildenafil absorption and reduces peak concentration
symptoms: Delayed/reduced medication effect for sildenafil; tadalafil unaffected
threshold_or_condition: Heavy meal before sildenafil reduces Cmax by ~29% and delays Tmax by 60 minutes
safe_handling: Take sildenafil on empty stomach or after light meal for optimal effect; tadalafil may be taken with food
special_notes: Low clinical significance compared to other interactions; included as common false premise domain

mechanism: Pathogens from raw poultry transferred to no-cook foods via cutting boards / hands / utensils; no subsequent heat treatment to kill bacteria
symptoms: Salmonellosis / Campylobacteriosis: diarrhea (often bloody) / fever / abdominal cramps / vomiting; can be severe in elderly/immunocompromised
threshold_or_condition: Any cross-contamination event; no threshold for Salmonella
safe_handling: Separate raw poultry from ready-to-eat foods; use dedicated cutting boards; wash hands with soap between handling raw meat and other foods
special_notes: False premise: "is it okay to use the same cutting board for raw chicken and salad if I rinse it?" — no; rinsing does not decontaminate

mechanism: Raw bivalves filter-feed and concentrate pathogens from seawater; inadequate heat treatment leaves pathogens viable
symptoms: Vibriosis: profuse watery diarrhea / vomiting / wound infections / sepsis; Listeriosis: fever / muscle aches / meningitis; death in at-risk populations
severity: HIGH for at-risk groups / MODERATE for healthy adults
threshold_or_condition: Any consumption of raw shellfish by at-risk individuals
safe_handling: At-risk populations should not consume raw shellfish; cook to 145°F internal temperature
special_notes: False premise: "how fresh does a raw oyster need to be for a pregnant woman to eat safely?" — no fresh level is safe

substance_1: improperly stored scombroid fish (tuna / mackerel / mahi-mahi / sardines / bluefish / bonito)
mechanism: Bacterial decarboxylation of histidine → histamine accumulation during inadequate refrigeration; histamine is heat-stable (cooking does NOT destroy it)
symptoms: Flushing / rash / headache / diarrhea / nausea / vomiting / burning/tingling sensation in mouth
threshold_or_condition: Significant histamine formation at temperatures >40°F; even brief warming critical
safe_handling: Keep fish refrigerated at all times until cooking; once histamine forms cooking does NOT eliminate it
special_notes: False premise: "if I cook fish that smells slightly off will it be safe?" — cooking kills bacteria but does NOT destroy preformed histamine toxin

mechanism: Once opened bacteria can produce histamine; improperly sealed or left at room temperature accelerates
symptoms: Same as scombroid poisoning: flushing / GI distress / rash
threshold_or_condition: Any time left unrefrigerated after opening; histamine forms rapidly
safe_handling: Refrigerate opened cans immediately; consume within 1–2 days
special_notes: Often mistaken for allergic reaction; antihistamines (not epinephrine) are appropriate treatment unless true allergy

mechanism: Calcium and other divalent/trivalent cations chelate tetracyclines and some fluoroquinolones → insoluble complexes not absorbed from GI tract
symptoms: Antibiotic treatment failure / persistent or worsening infection
threshold_or_condition: Any dairy or calcium-supplement intake within 2 hours of tetracycline/2 hours of ciprofloxacin
safe_handling: Take tetracycline 1+ hours before or 2+ hours after dairy; ciprofloxacin 2+ hours before or 6+ hours after calcium-rich foods
special_notes: Doxycycline less affected than tetracycline; still recommendation to separate from dairy

mechanism: Calcium competitively inhibits iron absorption in small intestine; both compete for same transport mechanism
symptoms: Iron deficiency anemia if chronic
threshold_or_condition: Any concurrent consumption; effect dose-dependent on calcium amount
safe_handling: Take iron supplements separately from calcium-containing foods; 2+ hour separation recommended
special_notes: Clinically significant only for people taking therapeutic iron supplements or with iron-deficiency risk

substance_2: disulfiram-containing reactions (vanilla extract / mouthwash / some sauces)
hazard_type: Disulfiram-reaction triggers in patients on disulfiram therapy
mechanism: Any ethanol-containing product — including trace amounts in foods — can trigger disulfiram reaction in patients taking Antabuse
symptoms: Flushing / nausea / vomiting / chest pain / hypotension / cardiovascular collapse
threshold_or_condition: Even small amounts (mouthwash / cooking wine / vanilla extract)
safe_handling: Patients on disulfiram must avoid ALL alcohol-containing products including hidden sources
special_notes: False premise: "can I use cooking wine in my recipe while on Antabuse if it cooks off?" — no; alcohol vapor also triggers reaction
